cases.title: No Chest Rise
cases.presentation: During CPR you attempt bag-mask breaths on an adult, but the chest does not rise and you hear air leaking around the mask. You are holding the mask with one hand.
cases.question: What is the best corrective action?
options: A: Squeeze the bag much harder and faster.
B: Improve the seal (ideally a two-rescuer two-handed E-C clamp), reopen the airway, and re-attempt.
D: Increase the breath rate to compensate.
cases.explanation: Air leaking with no chest rise means a poor seal or an unopened airway. Switching to a two-handed E-C clamp (with a second rescuer squeezing the bag) and reopening the airway fixes the leak far better than forcing more air, which only causes gastric inflation.
cases.clinicalPearl: Bag-mask is a two-person skill — a solid two-handed seal beats a hard squeeze every time.

cases.title: Trauma Airway
cases.presentation: A young man is unresponsive after a fall from a ladder, with a possible neck injury. He has a pulse but is breathing inadequately and needs assisted ventilation.
cases.question: How should you open his airway and ventilate?
options: B: Jaw-thrust without head extension and bag-mask ventilation, ideally with two rescuers, 1 breath every 6 seconds.
C: Hyperextend the neck to open the airway.
cases.explanation: With a suspected cervical injury, open the airway using a jaw-thrust that avoids neck extension. He has a pulse but inadequate breathing, so provide rescue breathing at 1 breath every 6 seconds using a bag-mask — not compressions.
cases.clinicalPearl: Pulse present + poor breathing = rescue breathing, not compressions; suspected neck injury = jaw-thrust, not head-tilt.

cases.title: Over-Bagging Under Stress
cases.presentation: A nervous new provider is bagging an intubated patient during a code, squeezing the bag rapidly about 25 times per minute. You notice the stomach is distending and the monitor shows the blood pressure generated by compressions is dropping.
cases.question: What should you advise?
options: A: Bag even faster to raise oxygen levels.
B: Slow to about 1 breath every 6 seconds (~10/min) with gentle volumes to visible chest rise.
cases.explanation: Rapid, forceful bagging over-ventilates the patient, raising intrathoracic pressure, reducing venous return and cardiac output, and inflating the stomach. Slowing to about 10 breaths per minute with gentle volumes restores effective circulation.
cases.clinicalPearl: In a code, the instinct is to bag too much — a calm 'one breath every six seconds' cadence protects the circulation.
